slirted sister team, the knociabout
comedians and the airily-arrayed, mer-
~ chorus girls in the spicy burletta
had worked assiduously to please the
many patrons, several of whom had
been attracted to the place by the
announcement that the bill would be
marked by grand temperance finale.


The vaudeville performers were glad
when the time came for them to take
the other side of the footights.


TIL after performance started prompt
~. the audience applauding the OK
horters and cheering the hymn sing
ing, all the time drinking beer and
other liquor ut tables scattered through
out the hall. Mrs. jain Bolton of
this city, who had charge of the per-
Torrance, joined with her associates
in singing the Battle Hymn of the
Republic" and asked the audience 20
join in the chorus. This they did with
vim, shouting the "Glory, glory, halle
lujah," in manner which showed that
it was an old-timer to them.


The singing was followed by a revival
speech by Mrs. Bolton. She thunder
ed against saloons and other drinking
places. The crowd cheered this and
Mrs. Bolton added that she was afraid
of neither man nor the devil. This
statement indicated 20 strongly how
great was the woman's bravery that it
brought more applause from the beer
drinkers and the women in the gal
lery.


Mrs. Bolton then introduced her sis
ter, Mrs. l.. Palmer of Elgin, Pa, who
led in a song service. made up of old
fashioned revival hymns, in which most
of those present joined heartily.


At the close of the performance one
of the revivalists said: This is the
way to get the men. am sure we
have driven home few nails at least -


Asked after the show whether t
was true that the revivalists had about
converted him to the idea of perma-
nently Transforming his music hall into
a church, Fenton said: Stranger things
have come to pass. have told these
people to go ahead and if they can
turn any man or woman from drinking
am glad of it, They can have my
place once week"
